Only tool: `python`. It receives `current_frame`, `previous_frame`, `history`, `transitions`, `last_transition`, `valid_actions`, `last_action_result`, and `action(actions)`. It also provides `last_animation` (per-action frames of your last `action(...)` call) and `frame_stats` (how much this game has animated so far) -- optional; use them only if you judge the intermediate motion informative rather than incidental.
Only letter-coded board views and lightweight metadata are exposed; raw numeric color IDs are not available.
Keep tool output compact: use `current_frame.segmentation` as the primary view, and `current_frame.ascii` only for a small specific region; never print full boards.
For the most recent change, compare `previous_frame` to `current_frame`, or `last_transition.before_frame` to `last_transition.after_frame`; `history[-1].frame` is the current frame, not the previous one.
Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.
Below you are provided with the current world model from the previous turn. The default behavior is to copy it and add or remove things based on the evidence that you gathered. BEFORE EXECUTING NEW ACTIONS YOU MUST ALWAYS GIVE THE REVISED VERSION OF THE WORLD MODEL.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it, but stop immediately if a result reports `game_over`, `level_completed`, or `done`.

- World model: SPACE "collected" the gray box above the player - the dark gray frame turned white. The white strip above the player disappeared. This suggests SPACE interacts with adjacent objects. Goal likely involves collecting all 3 gray-framed blue boxes. Player at rows 41-43, cols 32-35. Two gray boxes remain: left at (28-31, 16-19) and right at (24-27, 44-47).
- Plan: Test LEFT/RIGHT movement to navigate to the remaining boxes.
- Revise any item above immediately if `current_frame` or `history` contradicts it.
end of world model.

Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content: LEFT moved the player. Let me check the new position and see if the blue square (from the collected box) moved with the player. 65 cells changed is a lot - the player moved and something else changed too.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Check current state after LEFT
seg = current_frame.segmentation
print("Current nodes:")
for n in seg['nodes']:
    if n['color'] in ['c', 'b', 'N', 'W', 'g']:
        print(f"  Node {n['id']}: color={n['color']}, pixels={n['pixels']}, boundary={n['boundary']}")

# Check player position
for n in seg['nodes']:
    if n['color'] == 'N':
        print(f"\nPlayer: rows {n['boundary'][0][0]}-{n['boundary'][2][0]}, cols {n['boundary'][0][1]}-{n['boundary'][2][1]}")
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
OK, so after LEFT:
- Player moved from cols 32-35 to cols 28-31 (moved left by 4 columns)
- The white block (node 7) also moved from cols 32-35 to cols 28-31 - it moved WITH the player!
- The blue square (node 8) inside the white block also moved with it
So the white block (which was the collected gray box) is now attached to the player and moves with it. This is interesting - the player is carrying the collected item.
Now I need to figure out how to collect the remaining two gray boxes:
- Left box: rows 28-31, cols 16-19
- Right box: rows 24-27, cols 44-47
The player is at rows 41-43, cols 28-31. The white block (carried item) is at rows 36-40, cols 28-31.
To collect the left box, I need to get below it (since SPACE collected the one above the player). The left box is at rows 28-31, cols 16-19. I need to position myself at rows ~36-39, cols 16-19 (below the left box) and press SPACE.
But first, let me check if I can move through the blue bar at rows 28-31, cols 28-39. The player is at rows 41-43, cols 28-31. Moving UP would hit the carried white block at rows 36-40.
Wait - the white block is now at rows 36-40, cols 28-31. The player is at rows 41-43, cols 28-31. So the white block is directly above the player.
To get to the left box, I need to move LEFT more to align with cols 16-19, then move UP to get below the box.
Let me try moving LEFT again to get closer to the left box.

sections.label: ASSISTANT
sections.content:
World model: LEFT moves player left by 4 columns. The collected white block (carried item) moves with the player. Player at rows 41-43, cols 28-31; carried white block at rows 36-40, cols 28-31. Two gray boxes remain: left at (28-31, 16-19) and right at (24-27, 44-47). SPACE collects the box directly above the player.
Plan: Navigate left to align with the left gray box, then move up below it and press SPACE to collect it.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Move LEFT to align with the left gray box at cols 16-19
# Player is at cols 28-31, need to get to cols 16-19
# That's 12 columns left, or 3 LEFT actions (4 cols each)
# But first let me check if there are obstacles
# The left gray box is at rows 28-31, cols 16-19
# Player needs to be below it at rows ~36-39, cols 16-19

# Move LEFT twice to get to cols 16-19 (from 28-31, need 12 cols left = 3 moves)
# But first let me check if I can move up - the carried block is above
# Let me try moving LEFT once more
action(['LEFT'])
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>
